questions.q: Explain the concept of 'First Past The Post' (FPTP) system of election. Discuss its merits and demerits in the context of Indian democracy.
questions.answer: The First Past The Post (FPTP) system is a single-member plurality voting system used to elect members of the Lok Sabha and state legislative assemblies in India. In this system, the candidate with the most votes wins the election. The merits of FPTP include simplicity, speed, and ease of understanding. It is also a tried and tested method that has been in use for centuries. However, the demerits of FPTP include the possibility of a candidate winning with less than 50% of the votes, the lack of representation for minority groups, and the potential for tactical voting. In the context of Indian democracy, FPTP has been criticized for leading to a two-party system and the marginalization of smaller parties. Nevertheless, it remains the dominant electoral system in India due to its simplicity and ease of administration.
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of FPTP system (1 mark)
Discuss its merits (1 mark)
Discuss its demerits (1 mark)
Analyze its impact on Indian democracy (2 marks)

questions.q: Discuss the concept of 'Proportional Representation' (PR) system of election. Explain its advantages and disadvantages in the context of Indian democracy.
questions.answer: The Proportional Representation (PR) system is a voting system used to elect members of the Rajya Sabha and the state legislative councils in India. In this system, seats are allocated to parties based on the proportion of votes they receive. The advantages of PR include greater representation for minority groups, the ability to elect candidates from smaller parties, and the promotion of coalition politics. However, the disadvantages of PR include complexity, the potential for fragmentation, and the lack of accountability of elected representatives. In the context of Indian democracy, PR has been criticized for leading to instability and the lack of a clear majority. Nevertheless, it remains an important part of India's electoral system due to its ability to promote diversity and representation.
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of PR system (1 mark)
Discuss its advantages (1 mark)
Discuss its disadvantages (1 mark)
Analyze its impact on Indian democracy (2 marks)

questions.q: What is the significance of the Election Commission of India (ECI) in the context of Indian democracy? Discuss its powers and functions.
questions.answer: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an independent constitutional body responsible for conducting free and fair elections in India. The significance of ECI lies in its ability to ensure the integrity and impartiality of the electoral process. The powers of ECI include the delimitation of constituencies, the registration of voters, and the conduct of elections. The functions of ECI include the preparation of electoral rolls, the appointment of observers, and the disposal of election petitions. The ECI also has the power to issue Model Code of Conduct, which outlines the dos and don'ts for political parties and candidates during elections. Overall, the ECI plays a crucial role in promoting democracy and ensuring the smooth conduct of elections in India.
marking_scheme: Explain the significance of ECI (1 mark)
Discuss its powers (1 mark)
Discuss its functions (1 mark)
Analyze its impact on Indian democracy (2 marks)

questions.q: Discuss the concept of 'Right to Vote' in the context of Indian democracy. Explain its importance and the measures taken to ensure its exercise.
questions.answer: The Right to Vote is a fundamental right enshrined in the Indian Constitution. It is the ability of citizens to participate in the electoral process and choose their representatives. The importance of Right to Vote lies in its ability to promote democracy, accountability, and representation. The measures taken to ensure the exercise of Right to Vote include the compulsory registration of voters, the use of electronic voting machines (EVMs), and the provision of facilities for persons with disabilities. Furthermore, the Election Commission of India (ECI) has implemented various initiatives to increase voter turnout, such as the National Voters' Day and the Systematic Voters' Education and Electoral Participation (SVEEP) programme. Overall, the Right to Vote is essential for the functioning of Indian democracy, and efforts are being made to ensure its exercise by all eligible citizens.
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of Right to Vote (1 mark)
Discuss its importance (1 mark)
Discuss the measures taken to ensure its exercise (1 mark)
Analyze its impact on Indian democracy (2 marks)

questions.q: What is the concept of 'Electoral Reforms' in the context of Indian democracy? Discuss its need and the measures proposed to bring about electoral reforms.
questions.answer: Electoral Reforms refer to the changes and improvements made to the electoral system to make it more democratic, inclusive, and accountable. The need for electoral reforms in India arises from the perceived shortcomings of the existing system, such as the lack of representation for minority groups, the dominance of money and muscle power in elections, and the lack of transparency in the electoral process. The measures proposed to bring about electoral reforms include the use of proportional representation system, the introduction of not-a-vote (NOTA) option, the increasing of the minimum age for voting, and the use of technology to improve the electoral process. Furthermore, the government has set up the Law Commission and the Election Law Review Committee to examine and recommend electoral reforms. Overall, electoral reforms are necessary to strengthen Indian democracy and ensure that it remains representative and accountable to the people.
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of Electoral Reforms (1 mark)
Discuss the need for electoral reforms (1 mark)
Discuss the proposed measures for electoral reforms (1 mark)
Analyze its impact on Indian democracy (2 marks)
